data.title: SGOV: Short End Still Looks Attractive As Fed Cuts
data.description:
The iShares 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:SGOV) does exactly what its name suggests. This ETF gathered $26 billion in assets under management (up from $17 billion last year). It serves to buy and hold U.S. treasuries with only a few months until maturity. It has a 0.07% expense ratio, which seems quite reasonable to me. Interest is distributed monthly. This ETF returned 4.43% since my last article, dating back to December 2023. Slightly disappointing but, in terms of real yield (after inflation) it did better than I expected at that time. Inflation has pretty much continued to come down.

In the article, I concluded: "Probably a lot of long bond positions will work in the near term, but I'm okay making 5.4% more or less risk-free."

Today, the yield isn't as high in a nominal sense. The curve clearly shifted. The front end is down quite a bit. At the longer end, it is down substantially compared to last year:

If you think about the return from a real return perspective, the front hasn't moved all that much.

However, I'd argue we're now closer to the point where we could see a resurgence in inflation. I don't mean to say it is likely to flare up anytime soon. Energy prices are low, inflation is trending down, and employment is now a larger concern to the Fed.

But the Fed has been signalling it will ease up on QT going back to June, and it is expected to continue to taper into 2025. Studies suggest QT is in some ways equivalent to hiking rates. It follows, less-QT has a stimulative effect.

The first real 50bp cut is now also in the rearview mirror. Everything is pointing towards a benign inflation path now, but increasingly it will take less and less kindling to reignite the fire.

The next Fed meeting is 16 days from now. The market is pricing in a ~90% probability of a 0.25 BP cut and a ~10% probability of no change (see CME FedWatch tool below):

I think the odds of no change should be slightly higher because the economic data coming out after the last Fed meeting wasn't all that bad (retail sales were solid) most importantly; jobs were surprisingly strong and geopolitics have (so far) not spiraled out of control further.

If I take all of this together, short-term government bonds still look like a relatively attractive proposition to me in the short to medium term. This should deliver 4.6% of nominal returns on an annualized basis. Meanwhile, there's no real credit risk at this point. I get that the 10-year at 4.18% is quite attractive as well. If rates are cut aggressively, the 10-year will look much better in retrospect. But if inflation resurfaces, the 10-year will get hammered. In the near term, it even suffered as rates were lowered. To trade at 4.18% the market figures the Fed is likely to be between 300-375 on the Fed funds rate at the end of next year:

In other words, the 10-year is more or less counting on something like 150bp of cuts. Not exactly, but that's how I think about it. This looks overconfident to me. Especially, given the recent data. I'm interested in locking in rates for longer at some point, but not right now. A 4.6% (likely declining) yield seems better.

Investment-grade bonds offer only a slight premium (trading at a yield of around 4.85%). These come with duration risk similar to bonds in addition to their credit risk.

High yield is a completely different risk, but rarely has the spread to treasuries been this low:

Asset classes I do kind of like at this point include equities, gold, and bitcoin. However, these have a completely different risk profile. If I wanted to have an allocation to a vanilla fixed-income option, I'd still favor the short end, even though the yield curve has shifted quite a bit since my last write-up.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 10/23/24
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

Readers interested in investing topics tied to the upcoming election can find more information at the upcoming Seeking Alpha Investing Forum: Election 2024; please visit the event website.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Enphase Energy: Sell On Weak Near-Term Outlook (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Note:

I have discussed Enphase Energy, Inc. (NASDAQ:ENPH) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier coverage of the company.

After the close of Tuesday's session, leading microinverter and battery storage solutions supplier Enphase Energy Inc. or "Enphase" reported another set of disappointing quarterly results with revenues and profitability coming in below consensus expectations:

However, reported results came in within the guidance ranges provided on the Q2/2024 conference call:

While revenues in the United States increased by approximately 43% on a sequential basis, sales in Europe were down by 15% relative to management's previously stated expectations of "a little down to flat".

With Enphase having entered additional European markets during the quarter, the magnitude of the sequential decline is eye-catching.

Even worse, sell-through of the company's products in Europe was down by 34% on a sequential basis, as outlined by management in more detail on the conference call:

The overall sell-through of our products in Europe was a little more down, 34% down in Q3 compared to Q2. While every country in Europe has its nuances, the overall business environment in the region is challenging. Power prices have declined from the early 2023-highs. The economic growth is slow and the consumer confidence is limited.

In addition, there continues to be regulatory uncertainty in the Netherlands, which has been one of Europe's largest markets in the past.

In France, expected utility rate cuts in early 2025 are likely to impact near-term demand.

Similar to last quarter, total sales benefited from a large increase in IQ Battery shipments:

During the quarter, battery sales got a one-time boost from increased channel shipments. However, with channel inventories now at healthy levels, this benefit won't repeat.

While micro-inverter sales were also up by more than 20% from the second quarter, the company is still operating at a tiny fraction of its global micro-inverter capacity, which currently stands around 7.25 million units per quarter.

During the quarter, Enphase generated $161.6 million in free cash flow and allocated $49.7 million to share buybacks:

Since inception of the company's up to $1 billion share repurchase program last year, Enphase has bought back approximately 3.69 million shares at an average price of $108.86. Remaining capacity under the program amounts to almost $600 million.

Enphase ended the second quarter with $1.77 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities, up from the $1.65 billion reported at the end of Q2.

Debt remained virtually unchanged at $1.31 billion:

Please note that the company might be required to redeem $102.2 million in convertible notes in cash on March 1, 2025. While this minor debt maturity won't be a problem, the company will have to address an additional $632.5 million in convertible notes twelve months later.

However, with solid cash generation likely to persist, redemption of the 2026 convertible notes should not be an issue either.

In the earning release, Enphase also provided its outlook for the fourth quarter. Disappointingly, management does not expect further sequential improvement in sales:

While the U.S. business is projected to show incremental improvement, demand in Europe is expected to slow down even further. In addition, IQ Battery shipments are expected to decrease slightly on a sequential basis.

Please note that the company's Q4 revenue outlook is well below consensus expectations:

Even worse, given persistent issues in Europe, current consensus expectations of 40%+ year-over-year revenue growth in 2025 will likely have to come down materially.

On the conference call, management was optimistic about prospects for the U.S. business next year but this is not likely to fully offset persistent weakness in Europe.

During the questions-and-answers session, management basically admitted to previously stated normalized quarterly revenue assumptions being too high, at least in the near-term:

Philip Shen

Yeah. Thanks for taking my questions. First one is on -- in the past, you've talked about getting to a $450 million to $500 million sell-through demand run rate. And back in May, you thought it might be sometime in Q4. What's your thinking now in terms of when you can hit that on a quarterly basis? Is it sometime through '25, maybe Q2? Or could it be more in the back half? Thanks.

Badri Kothandaraman

Yes. I mean, look we talked about that, and we have had a few things there. We have had a few installer bankruptcies. I mentioned a big one. And we've had some headwinds. While the US is proceeding in the right direction, Europe is an entirely different story. So basically, a few months back, we didn't anticipate the installer bankruptcies, one in the US, we didn't anticipate the European business declining further. And that's reality right now.

Consequently, I would expect analysts to adjust their models and reduce price targets across the board over the next couple of weeks.

With business conditions in Europe unlikely to improve meaningfully anytime soon, I would expect the company's shares to remain in the penalty box for at least the next couple of quarters.

Given this issue, I am downgrading Enphase Energy's shares from "Hold" to "Sell".

Bottom Line

Enphase Energy reported disappointing Q3/2024 results and issued Q4 guidance well below expectations as business conditions in Europe continued to deteriorate during the quarter.

While the company's core domestic market is expected to recover further going into 2025, persistent weakness in Europe is likely to impact Enphase Energy's growth trajectory for the time being.

As a result, I would expect analysts to reduce price targets across the board, with the stock likely to remain in the penalty box until Europe starts to show signs of improvement.

For my part, I would not be surprised to see the company's shares moving to new 52-week lows sooner rather than later.

Consequently, I am downgrading Enphase Energy's shares from "Hold" to "Sell".

data.title: TransMedics' Organ Care: Critical Innovation With Lofty Expectations
data.description:
TransMedics Keeps the Beat Going with Organ Care and Soaring Revenues

TransMedics Group (NASDAQ:TMDX) specializes in the handling and transportation of vital organs like the heart, lungs, and liver. Its Organ Care System [OCS] provides extracorporeal warm perfusion [EWP] and real-time organ assessment. This enables longer preservation and improved viability of donor organs. TransMedic's OCS has achieved FDA approval in lung, heart, and liver transplantation after pivotal trials like INSPIRE, PROCEED II, and PROTECT. Peer-reviewed publications have supported OCS benefits over conventional cold storage and transportation of organs. However, its main benefit surrounds its ability to "expand the feasible geographic distance between (...) procurement and transplantation," thus expanding the donor poor. Estimates for the global transplantation market range between $10 billion and $15 billion. In the warm perfusion technology market, the company faces competition from OrganOx and XVIVO Perfusion in liver and lung transplantation, respectively. TransMedics is just beginning to tap into this market. Q2 revenue surged to $114.3 million, up from $52.5 million in Q2 2023. The net income of $12.2 million represented a net profit margin of 10.7%. After launching its own aviation services as a part of its National OCS Program (NOP) in 2023, TransMedics reports revenues under two categories: service (logistics, transportation, recurring, lower margin) and product (selling the OCS device and disposable components used in each transplant).

After summarizing the market potential, moat, and impressive financial performance in the early innings of warm perfusion technology, it's time to discuss some risks and limitations.

TransMedics’ Warm Perfusion: Hot Innovation or Lukewarm Market?

For one, OCS is "very expensive" and complex relative to alternatives like cold storage. Importantly, its cost-effectiveness remains uncertain. For example, in a June 2024 study of 13,930 transplant patients, portable ex vivo lung perfusion was found to be "not currently cost-effective at low-volume transplant centers in the United States." Moreover, normothermic regional perfusion [NRP] is emerging as a "cost-effective alternative in donation." This method involves restoring circulation to donor organs within the body by maintaining blood flow at physiological temperatures at death. This reduces the need for expensive external devices and consumables like those required by the OCS. Admittedly, the process is also complex and raises ethical questions. According to my understanding, the ethical concern is whether NRP "re-establishes" neurological function after "legal and medical death" via collateral flow (clamps are placed procedurally and strategically to prevent direct flow to the brain), potentially resulting in unnecessary suffering on the behalf of the donor. However, studies suggest no such incidences. It is estimated that around 11.4% of liver transplants come from donors using NRP, and there is a clear upward trend, although utilization is still relatively limited in the US. NRP can only be utilized in donation after circulatory death, which accounts for ~1/4 of all cases. Most donations are made after brain death.

According to a cost-utility analysis in the UK, OrganOx metra (for liver perfusion and transportation) is likely to be cost-effective and associated with better patient outcomes. Metra is approved in Europe and the U.S. and can preserve donated livers for up to 12 hours before transplantation, which is similar to OCS. OrganOx does not have a significant presence in the United States, so I do not expect it to "best" OCS in the EWP liver market.

The real question is how much demand will exist for EWP. My interpretation of the literature thus far suggests that EWP is useful as a "supplement," particularly in higher-risk cases with long geographical distances. In this niche, TransMedics has a considerable moat, especially as their services expand. Assuming EWP technology takes 25% of the total US transplant market (~20,000 annual transplants with livers making up the majority of organs) and an 80/20 market share between TransMedics and competition (e.g., OrganOx metra), this implies 4,000 annual TransMedics' transplants. This suggests that the company's TAM is approaching its halfway point.

Here are some key considerations.

  • TransMedics is not the only U.S. provider of EWP technology.
  • EWP is not definitively the preferred transplantation in all cases, but is best suited for high-volume centers where geographic reach is an important consideration.
  • NRP is more affordable, less complex, and possibly leads to better utility outcomes than alternative solutions, particularly for liver and heart transplants donated after circulatory death. Lending to its cost-effectiveness, it's also worth noting that NRP can preserve multiple organs at once due to the "simultaneous treatment of multiple organs."

TransMedics' Future Hinges on Sustained Growth and Profit Margins

I believe markets are generally efficient, making it crucial to understand the assumptions driving a stock's valuation. By identifying these assumptions, we can assess whether they are realistic and evaluate how key factors, such as changes in margins, may affect future returns.

Regarding margins, following the aviation acquisition, TMDX stock slumped considerably. Looking back, investors may have been concerned about the company's ability to turn profits from these operations. Although "services" growth has resulted in smaller profit margins, it is impressively already a profitable venture, buoying the stock.

TMDX trades at a P/E multiple [FWD] around 3x that of its peers but is also growing (2025) revenue 900% faster than its peers. TransMedics will not continue to grow as fast as it is now. As such, its P/E ratio will likely come down to earth. For TMDX to be a worthwhile investment at this price and given its risk, I believe that 2026-2029 revenue will need to grow at least 20%. The transplantation market by itself is growing at a CAGR of 8.5%, so this requires considerable growth in market share as well. 20% revenue growth would still support a high P/E multiple (i.e., 40) relative to the sector median of ~30.

To summarize, if TMDX can consistently achieve 20% annual revenue growth and 15% net profit margins, its expected returns warrant cautious investment ("hold"). TMDX becomes a "buy" if it becomes apparent they can achieve 25% revenue growth (estimated annual return bumps up to 14.3%) and/or better profit margins than 15% (e.g., a 20% net profit margin bumps estimated annual return to 17.2%). If TMDX cannot grow revenue at 20% and/or its net profit margins are lower than 15%, its stock is likely a "sell" at these prices. Coincidentally, Baird believes TMDX can achieve 25%+ revenue growth that supports an "outperform rating" with a price target for the stock at "$200."

Financial Health

As of June 30, TransMedics reported $362.751 million in cash. Total current assets were $509.265 million, while total current liabilities were just $54.329 million. This indicates a current ratio well over 2, which implies the company can readily cover any short-term obligations. TransMedics does owe $448.534 million in convertible senior notes and $59.217 million in "long-term debt."

In the first six months of the year, TransMedics generated $22.283 million from operations. Given the company's solid balance sheet and ability to generate profits, they shouldn't have any problems paying off their debt.

TMDX Stock: Risk/Reward Analysis and Investment Recommendation

TransMedics is doing an excellent job gaining market share in the $10 billion-plus transplant market. Their technology is important, and they are quickly establishing a niche in this market. Its financial and stock performance thus far have clearly indicated exceptional execution. However, investors should be aware of some potential risks. The most obvious is that TMDX is priced near perfection. The assumptions are becoming increasingly lofty, and it will be challenging, but not impossible, for management to meet them. Although EWP has advantages over cold storage in transplants, it is not yet widely used, and its cost-effectiveness is questionable. Furthermore, TransMedics faces competition in the EWP market, and NRP is an alternative solution that may be less expensive, simpler, and lead to better organ outcomes for up to 1/3 of all transplant cases (its utilization is expected to grow beyond 25%).

TMDX is a textbook Quadrant 1 investment (high risk, high reward) that could fit nicely into a barbell portfolio. Its high valuation, however, should be approached with caution ("hold"). I believe TMDX is fairly valued by the market, assuming it continues to experience robust revenue and net profit margin growth.

data.title: CB Leading Economic Index Continues To Fall In September
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The latest Conference Board Leading Economic Index (LEI) decreased in September to its lowest level since October 2016. The index fell 0.5% from the previous month to 99.7, marking its seventh consecutive monthly decline.

“Weakness in factory new orders continued to be a major drag on the US LEI in September as the global manufacturing slump persists,” said Justyna Zabinska-La Monica, Senior Manager, Business Cycle Indicators, at The Conference Board. “Additionally, the yield curve remained inverted, building permits declined, and consumers’ outlook for future business conditions was tepid. Gains among other LEI components were not significant enough to offset weakness among the four gauges mentioned above. Overall, the LEI continued to signal uncertainty for economic activity ahead and is consistent with The Conference Board expectation for moderate growth at the close of 2024 and into early 2025.“ More

Background on the Conference Board Leading Economic Index® (LEI)

The LEI is a composite index of several indicators. It is a predictive variable that anticipates, or leads, turning points in the business cycle and anticipates where the economy is heading. Since the LEI is comprised of multiple components, it is meant to provide a clearer picture as it is able to smooth out volatility associated with individual components.

The ten components of Conference Board LEI include: Average weekly hours in manufacturing; Average weekly initial claims for unemployment insurance; Manufacturers’ new orders for consumer goods and materials; ISM® Index of New Orders; Manufacturers’ new orders for nondefense capital goods excluding aircraft orders; Building permits for new private housing units; S&P 500® Index of Stock Prices; Leading Credit Index™; Interest rate spread (10-year Treasury bonds less federal funds rate); Average consumer expectations for business conditions.

Here is a chart of the LEI series with documented recessions as identified by the NBER. Note the peaks of the index preceding each of the recessions and the troughs occurring near the end of each recession.

Leading Economic Index and Recession Risk

For a better understanding of the relationship between the LEI and recessions, the next chart shows the percentage off the previous peak for the index. We are currently 17.6% off the 2021 peak.

The chart also calls out the number of months between the previous peak and official recessions. On average, there is usually 10.6 months between a peak and a recession. We are currently 33 months off from the 2021 peak.

Leading Economic Index and Its 6-Month Smoothed Rate of Change

Based on suggestions from Neile Wolfe of Wells Fargo Advisors and Dwaine Van Vuuren of RecessionAlert, we can tighten the recession lead times for this indicator by plotting a smoothed six-month rate of change to further enhance our use of the Conference Board's LEI as a gauge of recession risk.

The LEI has historically dropped below its 6-month moving average (of 6-month ROC) anywhere between 2 to 15 months before a recession. Currently, the LEI has been below its 6-month moving average (of 6-month ROC) for 27 months. Note that there have been times where the LEI has been below the 6-month for multiple months without a recession.

Here is a 12-month smoothed out version, which further eliminates the whipsaws:

Currently, the 12-month moving average (of 12 month ROC) has been negative for 21 months.

Conference Board Coincident Economic Index®

The Conference Board also includes its coincident economic index (CEI) in each release. The CEI measures current economic activity and is made up of four components: nonagricultural payroll, personal income less transfer payments, manufacturing and trade sales, and industrial production.

The Conference Board Coincident Economic Index® (CEI) for the US inched up by 0.1% in September 2024 to 112.9 (2016=100), after a downwardly revised 0.2% increase in August. The CEI increased by 0.9% in the six-month period ending September 2024, higher than its 0.5% growth rate over the previous six-month period. The CEI’s component indicators—payroll employment, personal income less transfer payments, manufacturing and trade sales, and industrial production—are included among the data used to determine recessions in the US. Payroll employment, personal income less transfer payments, and manufacturing and trade sales contributed positively to the index in September and slightly more than offset a decline in industrial production.

Observations show that when the LEI starts declining, the CEI continues to rise. The LEI has declined or been flat each month since April 2022. Over that same time frame, the CEI experienced only 5 monthly declines. Here's a chart including both the CEI and LEI.

Here is a chart of the LEI/CEI ratio, which perhaps has been a leading indicator of recessions. I count the lead time as the number of months that the ratio has been declining prior to a recession. The LEI/CEI ratio has now declined for 30 consecutive months. There have been times where the ratio has been in decline for several months without a recession.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Blackstone: I Think It's Time To Sell
data.description:
As usual, the earnings season of alternative-focused asset managers started with Blackstone Inc. (BX) reporting earnings on Thursdays. In September, the stock recovered to its all-time high level after a drawdown duration of more than a thousand days from ATH. With a post-earnings performance of 7.92%, Blackstone's stock finally surpassed the $160 mark and has accumulated a YTD performance of approximately 31.65%.

As you can observe from the title, I believe it's time to take some profits and sell Blackstone at its all-time high. I will lay out my primary reasons during the analysis. However, remember that this downgrade is primarily due to a valuation aspect and not because I am not bullish on the long-term prospects of a reputable brand such as Blackstone.

What Happened With Blackstone's Q3 Earnings?

In Q3, all operating metrics were better than analysts expected. First, total segment revenue of $2.43 billion exceeded expectations by 3%. Next, fee-related earnings and distributable earnings of $0.96 and $1.01 surpassed analyst forecasts by 2.1% and 11.0%, respectively.

In addition, net inflows of $40.5 billion were 13.1% more than analysts thought. Yet, although total AUM was in line with the consensus, Real Estate AUM was missed and was -4.7% lower than anticipated.

BX: Higher Consolidate Growth Rates

First, it is worth noting that Blackstone's top line is quite volatile due to the effect that carried interest brings to the fee composition. Considering that, in Q3, the firm experienced less impressive growth than in other quarters. Here, the growth of total segment revenues of 4.8% YoY was decent but lower than in Q2 '24 and Q4 '23. Furthermore, FRE grew 4.5% yearly and distributable earnings at 5.5%, becoming the highest DE growth since the metric started recovering in Q4 '23.

Nonetheless, what drives investors' attention is Blackstone's consistency in bringing new inflows quarter over quarter. In Q3, BX obtained an inflow-to-AUM ratio of 3.8%, consistent with the two-year average. Finally, from a growth perspective, fee-earning AUM moved 11.7% YoY, the highest number of the previous quarters.

All the performance metrics mentioned above are non-GAAP and are definitely the best ones to measure the performance of an alternative asset manager. Yet, what explained the rally on the stock was a surge in unrealized gains, mainly from performance allocations of $1.155 billion. This represents a 200.1% yearly rise in investment income, making it the highest in the last three years. In addition, there was a 4.9% dividend increase based on a policy comprising payouts of 85% of the distributable earnings.

These unrealized gains are counted as revenue under GAAP. Therefore, total revenue increased 44.1% YoY. Simultaneously, net income attributable to common shareholders grew 41.5% YoY. Although earnings came in spectacularly from this point of view, I don't think paying too much attention to unrealized gains is a prudent tool for measuring ongoing performance.

Blackstone's Results Per Segment; Private Credit Keeps Leading

Examining the earnings segment by segment, Credit & Insurance continued their elevated growth year over year, to the point that credit-heavy alternatives such as Ares Management Corporation (ARES) and Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL) jumped in Thursday's trading by 3.21% and 4.92%, respectively. Here, the segment achieved $324.5 million in fee-related earnings, and since Q1, it has become Blackstone's second-largest earnings source.

Real Estate continued its YoY decline in FRE earnings of -8.3%, becoming the only segment decreasing but still weighting most of BX's FRE. Next, Private Equity grew fee-related earnings at an attractive rate of 9.05% YoY, and Multi-asset grew FRE at a higher rate of 13.3%.

From the fee-paying AUM front, Credit & Insurance is obviously the segment that exhibited the largest YoY rise and is just $33.9 billion away from surpassing Real Estate in FPAUM. In terms of total AUM (which includes AUM not paying fees), Credit & Insurance is already the most significant AUM contributor to Blackstone, given the spectacular secular momentum.

Overvaluation of Blackstone

Blackstone's most recent closing price was $172.36. Summing each fee-related earnings of the past four quarters results in a TTM FRE of $3.68 per share, providing a price-to-FRE of 46.8x. Without a second of a doubt, the stock price is overvalued based on that multiple.

In my previous analysis of Blackstone, I downgraded them to a hold due to the valuation, which was significantly higher than the average. Back then, the price to FRE multiple was at 38.5x; now, it's even worse at 46.8x, making it completely unattractive when opening new positions.

From a DE standpoint, the TTM DE of $4.06 implies a price-to-DE of 42.5x, again completely off from a normalized level. The market seems to value Blackstone as if they were still enjoying the same earnings level from two years ago without considering the slowdown since then. For example, back in Q3 '22, TTM distributable earnings were at $5.81. Now, they are -30% lower at $4.06.

Take Away, Stock Rating, and Risks to the Rating

When analyzing Blackstone's key metrics, it is clear that growth rates have been decent in the top and bottom lines, with a particular emphasis on fee-earning AUM growth. This is while sitting at a normalized level of inflows to AUM, which is a crucial metric to measure the size of the inflows concerning how significant these are to the manager.

In the rating conclusion, I have two options.

  1. Rate the stock as a "hold" since the market is probably expecting the FRE growth to accelerate due to the substantial increase in NAV, and with this, the growing denominator would equate to a normalized valuation.
  2. Observe the current absurd valuation that factors a price-to-FRE of 46.1x, compare it to the exhibited growth, and conclude with a "sell" rating.

I will choose the second option here. Taking some profits sounds nice when the multiple expansion has reached a metric considerably higher than its average, and the stock is trading at all-time highs.

Nonetheless, Blackstone is a solid business that would most likely continue to benefit from secular trends of wealth managers' and institutions' allocating more capital towards alternative investments while enjoying the market leader attribute. In the long term, I do not doubt that this asset-light business model and the Blackstone brand are positioned to do well, even though fighting with the law of large numbers would start at some point due to their size.

In addition, although I opted for a "sell" rating due to their valuation, the market is the market, and so far, the stock is 68.61% up from a year ago and has exhibited a strong 7-day momentum of approximately 16%. Market sentiment is high, and taking profits now might have some opportunity cost if the stock price keeps rising. My rating is more of a tactical move rather than a rating with a five-year time horizon. I look forward to revisiting Blackstone's thesis once things in the valuation have normalized.

Furthermore, although fellow SA analyst Leo Nelissen decided on a "hold" rating on the stock, we agreed on the long-term outlook and valuation. From his side, he prefers to wait for a 15% pullback to find an attractive entry point to enter the stock. Finally, this week, competitor KKR & Co. Inc. (KKR) will report Q3 earnings on Thursday morning, and I look forward to reviewing their upcoming results.

data.title: RCM Technologies Q3 Preview: Grossly Undervalued, Even Under Conservative Estimates
data.description:
Introduction

RCM Technologies (NASDAQ:RCMT) is going to report Q3 ’24 earnings on November 6th, so I wanted to see what we should expect, give some comments on the company, and provide a valuation update. The company has been performing decently operationally speaking, while its share price has dropped by around 25% since the last time I covered it, which means the company is even more attractive than before; therefore, a strong buy rating is maintained. I will be adding more shares over the coming weeks, especially once Q3 numbers come out and if we don’t see anything too bad.

What to Expect from Q3

Analysts expect the company to earn $0.44 and $0.38 on an adjusted and GAAP basis, respectively, on $60.45m in sales. That is about 4% y/y growth in sales and -12% sequentially. So, analysts aren’t expecting a good quarter by the looks of it. Very little growth in the top line is expected, and a decrease in EPS. I wonder where these analysts, although there are only two that cover the company, got such low EPS estimates since the management is not guiding for Q3 at all. How likely is it the company will miss these expectations? Well, looking at the historical figures, in five out of the last eleven quarters, the company missed revenue estimates, some by quite a decent margin, while it only missed once on the EPS.

In my opinion, I expect the company to beat these, what I consider rather conservative, next quarter estimates. I would be very surprised if things go the wrong direction, and we see another plummet in share price.

What I would like to see

The company is slowly but surely getting away from that period of exuberance. The growth during the years of COVID-19 has been extraordinary and not comparable to today’s performance. If we strip out the effects of COVID-19, the company has been growing rather well. As per management comments in the Q2 earnings call, the company saw growth of around 19% in Healthcare and 23% in School revenue.

I would like to see this momentum continuing going forward and would like to see how the company’s school revenues are going to perform, which should be the busiest time of the year since the school year starts in September. The company is well on its way to adding another five school districts that bring in over $500k in revenues. The management seems rather optimistic that they will succeed in this mission; however, it is not given, so I will be looking out for some sort of clue in how that has developed and where that number is headed in the long run.

Gross margins have improved y/y, and I would like to see this continue in the next quarter, however, if we look at the previous quarter, there was an outsized growth in SG&A as compared to revenue growth, which meant that the improvement in gross margins didn’t trickle down to the bottom line at all. SG&A grew around 6% as compared to revenue growth of around 3%. Now, I would like to hear what the company has in store over the next few quarters to try and tackle the discrepancy, and with additional offices opening in lower-cost-of-labor countries like the Philippines, I would expect margins to continue to improve, but only time will tell.

Looking at the Engineering segment, we can see there was decent growth in Fixed Fees compared to last year, driven by growth in Energy Services, which is growing through some sort of a growth phase. I would like to see this carried forward and hope that the company takes advantage of the overall growth in the segment. Last quarter, the growth that the Engineering segment saw was around 24% y/y, so I would like to see similar numbers in Q3.

In the past, a lot of investors took an issue with the company’s receivables. It seems that the company has been slow at collecting the money that they are owed. It looks like it has been trending down the last quarter, so I would like to see this trend continue going forward.

Another item that I would like to see continue to come down is the company’s debt pile. It has increased 4-fold since the last time I covered the company, but in the last six months, the company has paid down $7m of the debt outstanding, so it is getting much more manageable. The company’s interest expense has been quite large since the debt it took on was floating and averaged at around 6.8%; however, now that the company has paid some of it down and that the interest rates have seen their first cut, it should be a lot easier to manage going forward.

Let’s look at an updated valuation.

Valuation

I’m going to keep it conservative as usual. For revenues, I went with a 5% CAGR over the next decade, which is much slower growth than its 3-year CAGR up to ’23, which was 13.6%.

For margins, the company is confident in improving them, however, to keep it conservative, I went with the margin profile that is forecasted for FY24, which means the company is not going to improve any further, and that’s a conservative take.

The company’s WACC is around 6.5%, but I thought it was a little low and not conservative enough; therefore, I went with a 10% discount rate to really keep these assumptions down. Additionally, I went with a 10% discount on the final intrinsic value calculation. With that said, RCMT’s intrinsic value is around $31 a share, which means it is trading at a decent discount to its (conservative) fair value.

Risks

Weakness in certain segments like Life Sciences and IT may keep the company’s share price down.

The company is facing a lot of competition in the segments it operates, which means they need to keep on top of the game to grow.

Speaking of growth, the low top-line performance is not appealing to many investors, which means it needs to step up its game to attract more investors and coverage.

The company needs to maintain the gross margin improvements going forward and lower operating expenses considerably so that all that margin improvement trickles down to the bottom line.

Closing Comment

Nothing has changed my outlook on the company in the last seven months since I covered it. Even with quite conservative estimates (in my view), the company is grossly undervalued; therefore, I am maintaining a strong buy rating.

I have been adding to my position over the last while and will continue to add slowly to get my average price down even further. So far, it is still a rather small position, and I am waiting to see what kind of numbers the company will put up in the next quarter and what the management has to say about next year if anything.
